reason: Both correctly identify the core judo (reframe criteria, attack complexity/cost, refuse to play the incumbent's game). Answer A is a tight, well-phrased summary but only implicitly touches segment/buyer targeting and doesn't mention getting ahead of the RFP/eval criteria or qualifying out of bad bake-offs. Answer B explicitly covers all four key points: reframing the axis, weaponizing breadth as liability, forcing a favorable metric into the eval, getting to the buyer/criteria before the formal bake-off (qualifying differently), and targeting the underserved buyer (developer vs. procurement) — each point is a distinct actionable lever rather than padding. B's added length is substantive, not filler, and it closes with the same crisp thesis as A. B is more decision-relevant and complete without sacrificing correctness.
